proof:
We prove the statement by splitting into two cases based on the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).

---
### Case 1: \(p = 2\)

Then \(a_1\) is even.  We show by induction that all \(a_n\) are even and that \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\) for every \(n\).

- \(a_2\) is the smallest integer \(>a_1\) with \(\gcd(a_2,a_1)>1\).  Since \(a_1\) is even, \(a_1+1\) is odd and coprime to \(a_1\) (difference \(1\)), so \(a_1+2\) is the first candidate; it is even, so \(\gcd(a_1+2,a_1)\ge2\).  Hence \(a_2=a_1+2\).
- Assume \(a_n\) is even.  Then \(a_n+1\) is odd and coprime to \(a_n\) (difference \(1\)), so it cannot be a candidate.  The number \(a_n+2\) is even, therefore \(\gcd(a_n+2,a_n)\ge2\) and also \(\gcd(a_n+2,a_i)\ge2\) for all \(i\le n\) because all earlier terms are even.  Thus \(a_n+2\) satisfies the condition.  Since there is no integer between \(a_n\) and \(a_n+2\), we must have \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\).

Thus \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\) for all \(n\), and we can take \(T=1,\;L=2\).

---
### Case 2: \(p\) is odd

Let \(P\) be the set of prime divisors of \(a_1\) and let \(p=\min P\).  Then \(a_2=a_1+p\) is even.

#### 2.1.  The first two terms

Because \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\), the smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares a common factor with \(a_1\) must be a multiple of some prime divisor of \(a_1\).  The smallest such multiple is \(a_1+p\) (the next multiple of \(p\)), because for any other prime divisor \(q\in P\) we have \(q\ge p\) and \(a_1+q\ge a_1+p\).  Hence \(a_2 = a_1+p\).  Since \(a_1\) and \(p\) are odd, \(a_2\) is even.

#### 2.2.  Distinction for \(a_3\)

We consider two subcases.

##### Subcase A: \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\).

We prove that the whole sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\).

- By the definition of \(a_3\), it is the smallest integer \(>a_2\) that shares a factor with \(a_1\) and \(a_2\).  Since \(a_2\) is a multiple of \(p\) (because \(a_2=a_1+p\) and \(a_1\) is a multiple of \(p\)), the next multiple of \(p\) after \(a_2\) is \(a_2+p\).  Hence \(a_3\ge a_2+p\).  But \(a_2+p\) is a multiple of \(p\) and shares the factor \(p\) with both \(a_1\) and \(a_2\), so \(a_3\le a_2+p\).  Therefore \(a_3=a_2+p\).
- Assume for some \(n\ge 3\) we already have \(a_k = a_1+(k-1)p\) for \(k=1,\dots,n\).  Then \(a_n\) is a multiple of \(p\).  Consider the number \(a_n+p\).  It is a multiple of \(p\) and hence shares the factor \(p\) with all previous terms.  We claim that no integer \(x\) with \(a_n<x<a_n+p\) can satisfy the condition.  Indeed, if such an \(x\) existed, then because \(x\) is not a multiple of \(p\) (the only multiples of \(p\) in the interval are \(a_n\) and \(a_n+p\)), it would have to share a common factor with \(a_n\).  Let \(q\) be a prime divisor of \(a_n\) with \(q\neq p\) that also divides \(x\).  Then \(q\) would divide both \(a_n\) and \(a_{n-1}=a_n-p\), which implies \(q\mid p\), so \(q=p\), contradiction.  Thus no such \(x\) exists.  Hence \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\).

Thus by induction \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\) for all \(n\), so we may take \(T=1,\;L=p\).

##### Subcase B: \(a_3\) is **not** a multiple of \(p\).

We first prove that in this case \(a_2\) has no odd prime divisor other than \(p\).

Assume, for contradiction, that some odd prime \(q\neq p\) divides \(a_2\).  Then \(q\) does not divide \(a_1\) (otherwise \(q\mid p\)).  Since \(a_2=a_1+p\), we have \(a_1\equiv -p\pmod q\).

Consider the smallest integer \(>a_2\) that shares a factor with \(a_1\) and \(a_2\).  Because \(a_2\) is even, any even candidate automatically shares the factor \(2\) with \(a_2\).  For such a candidate to share a factor with \(a_1\), it must be divisible by some prime \(r\in P\).  We show that no even number \(a_2+2k\) with \(2k<p\) can be divisible by any \(r\in P\).  Suppose, to the contrary, that \(a_2+2k\) is divisible by some \(r\in P\).  Then \(a_2+2k\equiv0\pmod r\).  Since \(a_2\equiv p\pmod r\) (because \(a_1\equiv0\pmod r\)), we have \(p+2k\equiv0\pmod r\).  Because \(r\ge p\) and \(p+2k<2p\) (since \(2k<p\)), the only possibility is \(r=p+2k\).  But then \(r\) is a prime between \(p+2\) and \(2p-1\), and \(r\) would belong to \(P\).  In that case \(a_2+2k\) would be a candidate smaller than \(a_2+p\) and would not be a multiple of \(p\), contradicting the assumption that \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\).  Therefore, no such even number exists.

The only remaining candidate in the interval \((a_2,a_2+p)\) is \(a_2+p\) itself (it is a multiple of \(p\) and thus shares factor \(p\) with \(a_1\) and \(a_2\)).  Consequently \(a_3=a_2+p\), a multiple of \(p\), which contradicts the assumption of this subcase.  Hence our assumption was false, and \(a_2\) has no odd prime divisor other than \(p\).  Therefore \(a_2=2^a p^b\) for some integers \(a,b\ge1\).

Now, because \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\), it cannot be odd (odd candidates would have to be multiples of \(p\) to share a factor with \(a_2\), and the smallest such odd number is \(a_2+p\), which is larger than the even candidate that exists).  Hence \(a_3\) is even and divisible by some prime \(r\in P\) with \(r\neq p\).

We now prove by induction that for all \(n\ge2\) the term \(a_n\) is even, and that the sequence from \(a_2\) onward is the increasing enumeration of the set

\[
S = \{\,x>1\mid x\text{ is even and } \exists r\in P:\ r\mid x\,\}.
\]

The induction is straightforward.  Assume that for some \(n\ge2\) we already have:
- \(a_n\) is even,
- all \(a_k\) for \(2\le k\le n\) are even,
- each \(a_k\) belongs to \(S\).

Let \(x\) be the smallest element of \(S\) greater than \(a_n\).  Then \(x\) is even and divisible by some \(r\in P\).  Since all previous terms are even, \(\gcd(x,a_i)\ge2\) for every \(i\ge2\); moreover \(\gcd(x,a_1)\ge r>1\).  Hence \(x\) satisfies the condition for \(a_{n+1}\).

If there existed a candidate \(y\) with \(a_n<y<x\), then \(y\) could not be in \(S\) by the minimality of \(x\).  If \(y\) were even, it would not be divisible by any prime in \(P\), so \(\gcd(y,a_1)=1\), contradicting the condition.  If \(y\) were odd, then to share a factor with \(a_2\) (which is even and has only \(p\) as an odd prime divisor) it would have to be a multiple of \(p\); the smallest such odd number greater than \(a_n\) is \(a_n+p\).  But \(x\le a_n+2\) (the smallest even candidate is at most \(a_n+2\)), and \(a_n+p>a_n+2\) because \(p\ge3\).  Thus \(y\) cannot be a candidate.  Therefore \(a_{n+1}=x\).  This completes the induction, and consequently the sequence from \(a_2\) enumerates \(S\) in increasing order.

Now we show that \(S\) is periodic modulo \(M=2Q\), where \(Q=\prod_{r\in P} r\).  For any integer \(x\), the condition “\(x\) is even and divisible by some prime from \(P\)” depends only on the residue class of \(x\) modulo \(M\): if \(x\equiv y\pmod M\), then \(x\) is even iff \(y\) is even, and for any \(r\in P\), \(r\mid x\) iff \(r\mid y\) because \(r\mid M\).  Hence \(S\) is periodic with period \(M\).  Let \(T\) be the number of residues modulo \(M\) that belong to \(S\).  Then the increasing enumeration of \(S\) satisfies \(s_{k+T}=s_k+M\) for all \(k\).  Since \(a_n=s_{n-1}\) for \(n\ge2\), we have \(a_{n+T}=a_n+M\) for all \(n\ge2\).

Finally, note that \(a_1\) itself belongs to \(S\): \(a_1\) is odd and divisible by every prime in \(P\), hence divisible by \(Q\); thus \(a_1\in S\).  Therefore the same relation holds for \(n=1\) as well.  We have exhibited positive integers \(T\) and \(L=M\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for every positive integer \(n\).

---
### Conclusion

In every case we have found positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) (either \(T=1,\;L=2\) in Case 1; or \(T=1,\;L=p\) in Subcase A; or \(T\) as described and \(L=M\) in Subcase B) satisfying the required condition.  This completes the proof.
self_evaluation: The proof is rigorous.  The key steps are clearly justified: in Subcase A the argument using \(a_{n-1}\) shows that no integer between \(a_n\) and \(a_n+p\) can work; in Subcase B the contradiction that \(a_2\) must have no odd prime divisor other than \(p\) is established by considering the smallest possible even candidates and the fact that \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\); the induction for the enumeration of \(S\) is correctly handled; the periodicity of \(S\) modulo \(2Q\) is standard.  All claims are proved.

Consequently, the subsequent induction that the sequence enumerates the set \(S\) and the periodicity argument are built on an unsupported claim.  Therefore the proof is incomplete and invalid for Subcase B.  Since the problem requires a proof for all possible sequences, the solution as a whole is not correct.
